steps.title: The pump screen at the petrol station

narration.default: Next time you're at a petrol station, look at the screen on the pump. Press the trigger and the number starts climbing — litres flowing into the tank. Let go, the number stops. Hit reset and it goes back to zero. Today you'll build that whole machine. One push button starts and stops the pump, another resets the total, and the live amount appears on a 16x2 LCD screen. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you are in Upload mode. If you have not done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.
Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace.

steps.title: Listen on pin 2 — the start button

narration.default: Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and change the mode to INPUT. Pin 2 is the start-and-stop button — the Arduino has to listen on it.

steps.title: Listen on pin 3 — the reset button

narration.default: Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 3 and keep the mode on INPUT. Pin 3 is the reset button — pressing it will zero the total.

steps.title: Tell the LCD which screen to talk to

narration.default: Open the 16x2 LCD category and drag a connect LCD block into setup. Leave the address on 0x27 and keep 16 chars by 2 lines. The LCD talks over a shared bus called I2C — many devices can sit on the same wires, and the address (0x27) is its house number on that bus.
Drag an initialize LCD block right after the connect LCD block. Two-step introduction: the first block named the screen, this one wakes it up so it's ready to print.

steps.title: Box one: is the pump running?

narration.default: Open Variables and click Make a Variable. Name it running. We'll use 1 for 'pump on' and 0 for 'pump off'. The pump remembers its own state between loops.

steps.title: Box two: how much has flowed

narration.default: Click Make a Variable again. Name this one flow. It holds the running total — how much fuel has gone through since the last reset.

steps.title: Box three: was the start button already held?

narration.default: One more variable. Click Make a Variable and name it lastStart. Same edge-detection trick as the people counter — without lastStart, holding the button for a second would flip the pump on, off, on, off many times. lastStart remembers what the button looked like last loop, so we only flip on a fresh tap.

Drag an if block into the loop. Build the condition: digitalRead pin 2 equals 1 AND lastStart equals 0. That means: the start button is pressed RIGHT NOW but it WASN'T pressed a moment ago — a brand-new tap, not someone holding the button down. That AND combo is edge detection.

Inside that if branch, drag a set running block. For the value, build 1 minus running. Cool maths trick: if running was 0, this makes it 1; if it was 1, this makes it 0. One tap flips the pump between off and on.

Below the first if, drag a second if block. The condition is digitalRead pin 3 equals 1. Inside, drag a set flow to 0 block. Pressing the reset button zeros the meter — exactly what the attendant does before each new customer.
Below the reset if, drag a third if block. Condition: running equals 1. Inside, drag a change flow by 1 block. Each loop while the pump is on, the meter advances by one — fuel flowing into the tank.

Below the third if, drop these in order from the 16x2 LCD category: clear LCD, set cursor to column 0 row 0, print Flow on LCD, then print number on LCD with the flow variable in the value slot. The screen wipes and redraws the live total every loop. Print-then-clear-then-print-fresh is how all rolling-number displays work.

steps.title: Save what the button looked like

narration.default: Below the print blocks, drag a set lastStart block. Drop a digitalRead pin 2 reporter into the value slot. lastStart now stores whatever the button looked like this round, ready for the edge check next time around. Without this, the edge trick would forget.

steps.title: A small pause to keep the screen calm

narration.default: Drag a delay block at the very bottom of the loop. Set it to 100 milliseconds. Without this gentle pause the LCD would flicker as we redraw it dozens of times per second. Ten redraws a second is plenty for a smooth count.

Open the Simulator and click the plus button. Choose LCD 16x2 and click Save. The display is wired through the I2C bus on SDA pin A4 and SCL pin A5 — those two wires carry data and clock for every I2C device.
Click the plus again. Add a Push Button on pin 2 — this is the start-and-stop button.
Click the plus once more and add a second Push Button on pin 3. That's the reset button. Both buttons are now wired.
Click Start. Your program compiles and the LCD lights up showing Flow 0.

Click the start button — the number on the LCD starts climbing. Click it again to pause. Click the reset button to drop the meter back to zero. The pump always remembers its state because of the running variable. Try this: change the change flow by 1 to change flow by 5 to make it count faster, like a fast pump nozzle. Or print the price on the second LCD line by multiplying flow by your local rate.
